clusters.title: Supreme Court Hears Arguments on Birthright Citizenship Case
clusters.summary: The Supreme Court is currently deliberating a significant case regarding birthright citizenship, with President Trump making history as the first sitting president to attend the arguments. This case could have far-reaching implications for immigration policy and citizenship rights in the United States. The discussions center on the legal interpretations of the 14th Amendment and its application to children born to non-citizen parents.

clusters.title: Trump Threatens Military Action Against Iran in Coming Weeks
clusters.summary: Former President Donald Trump has warned that the U.S. will take significant military action against Iran within the next two to three weeks. This statement has raised concerns in global markets, impacting stocks, bonds, and oil prices. The warning reflects ongoing tensions between the U.S. and Iran, emphasizing the potential for escalating conflict in the region.

clusters.title: NASA's Artemis II Mission Begins Journey to the Moon
clusters.summary: NASA's Artemis II mission has launched with four astronauts aboard, marking the first human journey toward the moon in over 50 years. The crew, consisting of three Americans and one Canadian, will not land on the lunar surface but aims to pave the way for future lunar exploration. This 10-day mission is a significant step in NASA's efforts to return humans to the moon and eventually establish a sustainable presence there.

items.title: Artemis II astronauts prepare to leave Earth’s orbit and head towards the moon
items.description:
Nasa mission enters its second day, with crew hoping to become first people to get close to the moon in over 50 years

Four astronauts are preparing to leave Earth’s orbit and slingshot towards the moon as Nasa’s Artemis II mission enters its second day.

The high-stakes 10-day voyage will mark the first time in half a century that humans leave space close to Earth and return to the vicinity of the moon. It is a crucial test of Nasa’s ambition to land humans back on the lunar surface this decade, and stay there permanently.

Continue reading...

items.description: What are the mission's four astronauts doing and when will they go to the Moon?

items.title: Artemis II Completes First Day of Its NASA Lunar Mission
items.description: The crew, three Americans and a Canadian, are the first humans to travel to the moon in more than 50 years. They will not land on the surface, but the mission will pave the way for future visits.

items.title: Artemis II Roars Into Space in Groundbreaking Moon Mission
items.description: The Artemis II, NASA’s most powerful rocket ever, blasted off with four astronauts inside the Orion space capsule on Wednesday, beginning their journey to get humans back on the lunar surface for the first time in more than 50 years. While this mission will not include a lunar landing, it will chart a path for humans to return to the moon in 2028. NBC’s Tom Costello reports and former NASA astronaut Mike Massimino joins TODAY with analysis, saying “A new era has really started.”

clusters.title: Trump Signs Executive Order to Limit Mail-in Voting
clusters.summary: Former President Donald Trump has signed an executive order aimed at exerting federal control over mail-in voting, a move that could impact the upcoming 2026 U.S. elections. The order has drawn criticism from leaders in states like California, who have vowed to challenge the new regulations. This development highlights ongoing tensions surrounding voting procedures and election integrity in the United States.

clusters.title: Federal Judge Blocks Trump's Order to Cut NPR and PBS Funding
clusters.summary: A federal judge has issued a permanent injunction against an executive order by former President Trump that sought to eliminate federal funding for National Public Radio (NPR) and the Public Broadcasting Service (PBS). The ruling was based on First Amendment protections, emphasizing the importance of public broadcasting in a democratic society. This decision is significant as it preserves funding for these media organizations, which play a crucial role in providing diverse news coverage.

clusters.title: Supreme Court Overturns Colorado's Conversion Therapy Ban
clusters.summary: The Supreme Court has ruled against Colorado's ban on conversion therapy, a practice aimed at changing an individual's sexual orientation or gender identity. The decision supports a Christian counselor who challenged the ban, highlighting ongoing debates over LGBTQ+ rights and the regulation of therapeutic practices. This ruling may have significant implications for similar laws in other states.

The move aims to manage the outflow while ensuring the funds can continue to operate effectively.

clusters.title: Refugee's Death at Buffalo Doughnut Shop Ruled Homicide
clusters.summary: The death of a nearly blind refugee from Myanmar, who was left by Border Patrol at a doughnut shop in Buffalo, New York, has been ruled a homicide by medical examiners. This incident, which occurred five days prior to the ruling, raises significant concerns about the treatment of vulnerable individuals in the immigration system. The case highlights ongoing discussions about border enforcement practices and their impact on refugees.

clusters.title: UK Hosts International Talks on Reopening the Strait of Hormuz
clusters.summary: The United Kingdom is convening over 40 countries to discuss strategies for reopening the Strait of Hormuz, a critical maritime route for global oil shipments. The talks aim to address security concerns and ensure safe passage through the strait, which has been a focal point of geopolitical tensions. The involvement of multiple nations highlights the importance of international cooperation in maintaining stability in the region.

clusters.title: Macron Criticizes Trump's 'Inelegant' Remarks on His Marriage
clusters.summary: French President Emmanuel Macron condemned remarks made by former U.S. President Donald Trump regarding his marriage, describing them as 'neither elegant nor up to standard.' The comments, which included Trump mocking Macron's accent during a private lunch, have sparked backlash in France, highlighting tensions between the two leaders.

items.title: Watch: Macron calls Trump's remarks on his marriage 'inelegant'
items.description: French President Emmanuel Macron has said Donald Trump's comments about his marriage were "neither elegant nor up to standard".

items.title: ‘Not up to standard’: Macron criticises Trump after comments about his marriage
items.description:
Anger in France after US president puts on French accent and mocks Macron during private lunch in Washington

  • Europe live – latest updates

Emmanuel Macron has said Donald Trump’s comments about his marriage were “neither elegant nor up to standard” after the US president put on an accent and mocked his French counterpart and his wife during a private lunch in Washington.

Arriving in South Korea on Thursday, Macron made clear his displeasure at Trump’s comments, which appeared briefly in a video on the White House YouTube channel before being removed.

Continue reading...

clusters.summary: President Trump has reportedly discussed the possibility of firing Attorney General Pam Bondi, though no final decision has been made. Speculation suggests that he may consider replacing her with Lee Zeldin, the current E.P.A. administrator. This potential change raises questions about the administration's legal strategies and priorities moving forward.

clusters.title: DHS Rescinds $100,000 Approval Rule to Expedite FEMA Relief
clusters.summary: Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin has rescinded a rule requiring personal approval for Department of Homeland Security expenditures exceeding $100,000. This change aims to streamline the funding process for FEMA relief efforts, potentially enhancing the agency's ability to respond to disasters more efficiently. The decision is seen as a significant step in improving disaster response capabilities amid ongoing recovery challenges.

clusters.title: Senate Approves Bipartisan Bill to Fund Most of DHS, Ending Shutdown
clusters.summary: The Senate has unanimously passed a bipartisan funding bill for most of the Department of Homeland Security, excluding Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol, effectively ending a 48-day government shutdown standoff. This legislative move comes as former President Trump appears to shift support towards the Senate's approach, reflecting a potential change in GOP dynamics regarding funding negotiations.

clusters.title: Libyan Leader Haftar Allegedly Acquires Drones Amid UN Embargo
clusters.summary: Satellite images suggest that Libyan military leader Khalifa Haftar has obtained at least three combat drones located at Al Khadim airbase, despite an existing United Nations arms embargo. clusters.title: US Lifts Sanctions on Venezuela's Acting President Delcy Rodríguez
clusters.summary: The United States has lifted sanctions on Delcy Rodríguez, the acting president of Venezuela, marking a significant step towards reestablishing diplomatic relations between the two nations. The move is seen as part of broader efforts to normalize ties amid ongoing political tensions.

items.title: US lifts sanctions on Venezuela’s acting president Delcy Rodríguez
items.description:
US moves towards reestablishing working relations between two countries after abducting President Nicolás Maduro

The US has lifted sanctions on Venezuela’s acting president, Delcy Rodríguez, in the latest step towards normalising relations between the two countries after US forces abducted her predecessor, Nicolás Maduro, and his wife.

The couple were taken to New York after their abduction in January to face charges of alleged drug trafficking, to which both have pleaded not guilty.

Continue reading...
